scenario: Agent is onboarding to a new OpenCode project and needs to verify ecosystem integrity. User says 'audit this project's OpenCode setup.'
expected_behavior: Agent loads hm-opencode-project-audit skill, compiles subagent bundles (scripts/compile-bundle.sh), validates structure (scripts/validate-skill.sh), then dispatches Phases 1-6 in parallel with appropriate specialist profiles.
pass_condition: Agent produces audit-report-YYYY-MM-DD.md with per-phase findings, cross-phase risk assessment, and evidence references. Output is fact-based, not prescriptive — no automatic fixes applied.

scenario: Agent is auditing a project that has non-standard OpenCode configuration (different config directory, custom agent locations). User says 'check if the agents in this project are configured correctly.'
expected_behavior: Agent applies the official OpenCode scope matrix before deciding what exists or is missing, checking both project-level (.opencode/) and global/config override locations.
pass_condition: Agent discovers non-standard configuration locations by checking OPENCODE_CONFIG_DIR, global config paths, and override locations. Audit report notes the actual configuration sources, not just .opencode/.

scenario: Agent is asked to fix issues found in an audit. User says 'the audit found some problems, can you fix them?'
expected_behavior: Agent follows the Iron Law ('AUDIT REPORTS FACTS. NEVER BLOCKS. NEVER FIXES.') and refuses to fix, instead providing remediation recommendations in the audit report.
pass_condition: Agent correctly identifies the boundary: auditing reports facts, fixing is a separate workflow. Refers to remediation recommendations rather than applying fixes directly.
